proposals.title: Beyond the Prescription Pad: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Legal Authority, Community Context, and Implementation Factors in Rural Pharmacist Service Adoption
proposals.motivation: The expansion of pharmacist scope of practice (e.g., for immunizations, point-of-care testing, naloxone dispensing) is a key policy lever to improve rural healthcare access. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests that legal authority alone does not guarantee service implementation, creating an 'implementation gap.' Understanding the relative influence of legal authority versus other contextual and operational factors is critical for designing effective support strategies for community pharmacies.
proposals.hypothesis: While necessary, legal authority is not the primary driver of pharmacist service adoption in rural settings. We hypothesize that a combination of pharmacy operational capacity, pharmacist self-efficacy, and perceived patient demand will be stronger predictors of service implementation than the presence of legal authority alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a national survey of independent community pharmacists in rural areas will measure the implementation of specific services (e.g., HIV testing, hormonal contraception), legal authority (state-level), and potential enablers/barriers (using validated scales for self-efficacy, workflow, reimbursement). Multivariate logistic regression will model service adoption. Second, qualitative interviews (informed by the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation) with a purposive sample of survey respondents will explore the decision-making process and contextual nuances behind service adoption or non-adoption.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, evidence-based model of service adoption that moves beyond a binary focus on legal scope. It will identify actionable, modifiable factors (e.g., communication training, billing support) that policymakers and pharmacy organizations can target to accelerate the uptake of patient care services in underserved rural communities.
proposals.novelty: This work integrates Westrick's prior focus on rural pharmacies, communication models, and implementation barriers to directly test the assumed primacy of legal authority, a largely untested assumption in pharmacy practice research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Beyond Reimbursement: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Internal Organizational and Cultural Barriers to Clinical Service Implementation in Rural Community Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Research on barriers to expanding pharmacy services (e.g., point-of-care testing, comprehensive medication management) in rural areas predominantly focuses on external factors like reimbursement and staffing shortages. However, persistent gaps in service adoption suggest that internal, pharmacy-specific factors may be equally or more influential. Understanding these internal dynamics is critical for designing effective implementation strategies that address the unique context of rural community pharmacy practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that internal factors, specifically pharmacy culture (e.g., attitudes toward clinical roles, perceived professional identity) and workflow design (e.g., task allocation, physical layout, technology integration), are greater barriers to implementing new clinical services in rural community pharmacies than the traditionally cited external barriers of reimbursement and staffing.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a national survey of rural community pharmacy managers will quantify the perceived impact of external vs. internal barriers using validated scales (e.g., Organizational Readiness for Change) and discrete choice experiments. Second, purposive sampling from survey respondents will select 8-10 pharmacies for in-depth, multi-day ethnographic case studies, including non-participant observation, workflow mapping, and semi-structured interviews with pharmacists and technicians. Qualitative data will be analyzed using a combined deductive (thematic analysis based on Normalization Process Theory) and inductive approach.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, systems-level understanding of implementation barriers, distinguishing between modifiable internal factors and less mutable external constraints. It will generate a novel framework for assessing pharmacy readiness and tailored implementation toolkits that address cultural and workflow redesign, moving beyond policy-focused recommendations. Findings will directly inform quality improvement initiatives and professional development programs for rural pharmacy teams.
proposals.novelty: While prior work catalogs external barriers, this study innovatively applies organizational theory and ethnographic methods to uncover the 'black box' of internal pharmacy operations and culture as a primary determinant of service implementation success, a perspective largely absent in the pre-2026 pharmacy services literature.

proposals.title: A Mixed-Methods Implementation Trial to Overcome Reimbursement, Documentation, and Staffing Barriers to Clinical Service Expansion in Rural Community Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Rural community pharmacies are critical access points for patient care, yet they face persistent implementation barriers (reimbursement complexity, documentation burden, staffing constraints) that limit the expansion of clinical services like point-of-care testing, immunization, and naloxone dispensing. These barriers exacerbate rural health disparities by restricting service availability. Overcoming them is essential for realizing the full potential of pharmacists as integrated care providers in underserved areas.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-component intervention, co-developed with pharmacy staff, that provides targeted tools for billing navigation, streamlined documentation, and workflow redesign will significantly reduce pharmacist-reported implementation barriers and increase the number of billable clinical services delivered over a six-month period, compared to provision of informational resources alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a hybrid type 2 effectiveness-implementation cluster randomized trial. Approximately 40 independent rural pharmacies will be randomized to intervention or active control (resource toolkit). The intervention arm will receive: 1) a tailored, interactive billing and reimbursement navigation algorithm (a decision-support software tool mapping payer-specific requirements to service codes), 2) standardized EHR-integrated documentation templates, and 3) practice facilitation involving workflow analysis and staff task-shifting protocols. Quantitative data (pre/post surveys on barrier severity, monthly service logs) will be analyzed using mixed-effects models. Qualitative data from post-trial interviews will be analyzed thematically to understand implementation mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first rigorously tested, practical intervention bundle specifically designed to address the interconnected financial, administrative, and operational barriers in rural pharmacy practice. It will provide a replicable implementation framework for expanding pharmacist-delivered care and produce actionable tools (e.g., the billing algorithm) that can be disseminated to the broader community pharmacy sector.
proposals.novelty: While prior surveys (including Westrick's) have cataloged barriers, this study moves beyond description to experimentally test a co-designed, multi-faceted solution. It uniquely integrates a technological tool for reimbursement navigation with practice facilitation, targeting the root causes of implementation failure rather than just provider education.

proposals.title: Quantifying Preferences for Community Pharmacy-Based Health Services in Rural Areas: A Discrete Choice Experiment with Pharmacists and Patients
proposals.motivation: Expanding community pharmacy services (e.g., point-of-care testing, immunizations, chronic disease management) is critical for improving rural healthcare access. However, successful implementation requires understanding which service features are most valued by the key stakeholders—pharmacists who must deliver services and patients who must utilize them. Pre-2026, there is limited quantitative evidence on the trade-offs these groups are willing to make regarding service attributes like cost, location, privacy, and pharmacist communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that both rural community pharmacists and patients have distinct, quantifiable preference structures for pharmacy service attributes, and that significant preference heterogeneity exists within these groups based on demographics and practice characteristics.
proposals.method: We will employ a discrete choice experiment (DCE), a quantitative conjoint analysis method from marketing. Two separate, web-based surveys will be developed: one for independent rural pharmacists (identified via national databases) and one for rural-dwelling patients. Each survey will present a series of choice tasks where respondents select their preferred service profile from competing bundles of attributes (e.g., service type, out-of-pocket cost, appointment mode, consultation time, result delivery method). Data will be analyzed using hierarchical Bayesian multinomial logit models to estimate part-worth utilities and relative importance scores for each attribute.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct comparison of pharmacist and patient utility functions for pharmacy service design in rural settings. It will generate actionable data for policymakers and pharmacy owners to prioritize service features that maximize uptake and satisfaction. The findings will extend implementation science by moving beyond identifying barriers to quantifying the value of potential solutions.
proposals.novelty: While DCEs are established in health economics, their application to align the implementation preferences of both pharmacy providers and rural patients is novel. This directly bridges Westrick's prior work on communication models, immunization systems, and service delivery modes with a robust marketing methodology to inform service design.

proposals.title: Geospatial Analysis of Community Pharmacy Immunization Sites to Identify Rural Coverage Deserts and Disparities in Access
proposals.motivation: Rural populations face significant barriers to accessing preventive health services like immunizations, a disparity highlighted during the COVID-19 pandemic. Pharmacies are critical access points, but their distribution may not align with areas of highest clinical and social vulnerability. A systematic, data-driven gap analysis is needed to inform public health policy and pharmacy service implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant geographic disparities exist, where areas with high population need (based on age, chronic conditions, and social vulnerability indices) have inadequate spatial access to pharmacy-based immunization services, and that these 'pharmacy deserts' are disproportionately located in rural communities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional geospatial analysis using pre-2026 U.S. data. Pharmacy locations offering immunizations will be sourced from national databases (e.g., NCPDP, state boards) and validated via survey. Population need will be modeled using CDC Social Vulnerability Index data, Medicare eligibility, and chronic disease prevalence at the census tract level. We will use ArcGIS or QGIS to perform network analysis, calculating drive-time service areas (e.g., 10, 20-minute thresholds) and applying two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) methods to quantify spatial accessibility. Gaps will be identified where high-need areas fall outside service radii.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first national, granular map of pharmacy immunization access gaps specific to rural populations. It will offer actionable intelligence for health departments and pharmacy organizations to target service expansion, mobile clinics, or partnership models. The methodology will establish a reproducible framework for ongoing monitoring of pharmacy-based care access.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacy desert concepts exist, this study innovatively integrates detailed immunization service verification with multi-dimensional vulnerability indices to define 'coverage deserts.' It moves beyond simple pharmacy presence to model actual service availability, a critical distinction for rural health equity.

proposals.title: Mapping Rural Pharmacy Referral Networks: A Social Network Analysis of Provider Connections and Patient Care Coordination
proposals.motivation: In rural communities, pharmacies are critical access points for health services, yet the structure and effectiveness of their referral networks to other providers (e.g., primary care, behavioral health) are poorly understood. Fragmented networks can create significant barriers for patients, especially for services like immunizations, naloxone access, and chronic disease management. Understanding these referral patterns is essential for improving care coordination and addressing rural health disparities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rural community pharmacy referral networks are sparse and centralized around a few key providers, and that stronger, more reciprocal network ties are associated with higher patient satisfaction and more effective service implementation (e.g., point-of-care testing).
proposals.method: We will conduct a national survey of independent community pharmacists to collect ego-network data on their referral relationships with physicians, clinics, and public health agencies. Using social network analysis (SNA), we will construct directed graphs and calculate metrics (density, centrality, reciprocity) using algorithms in UCINET or the `igraph` package in R. Regression models will then test associations between network characteristics (independent variables) and outcomes like service adoption and patient satisfaction scores (dependent variables).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative map of pharmacy-centered referral networks in rural America, identifying structural gaps and key influencers. It will offer actionable evidence for policymakers and practitioners to strengthen community-based care coordination, directly informing interventions to improve service delivery like immunization retrieval and naloxone distribution.
proposals.novelty: While SNA is used in health services research, its application to model the specific referral role of rural pharmacies from the pharmacist's perspective is novel. This moves beyond describing individual pharmacy barriers to analyzing the systemic connectivity that enables or impedes patient care.

proposals.title: Defining Referral Thresholds: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis to Identify Community Pharmacies Where Immunization Services Are Not Viable
proposals.motivation: While expanding pharmacy-based immunization is a public health priority, a one-size-fits-all approach risks patient safety and pharmacist burnout in settings with insurmountable barriers. Pre-2026 research by Westrick and others highlights significant variability in resources, staffing, and integration with systems like IIS, especially in rural and independent pharmacies. This study addresses the inverse, under-explored question: under what specific conditions should a pharmacy refer immunization services out rather than provide them?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a discrete, identifiable set of structural, operational, and community-level factors—when present in combination—define a threshold below which in-pharmacy immunization provision is neither safe, effective, nor sustainable, necessitating systematic referral.
proposals.method: We will conduct a national survey of community pharmacists, building on Westrick's prior survey methods, to collect data on structural (e.g., physical space, IIS access), operational (e.g., staffing, workflow), and community (e.g., patient volume, payer mix) variables. Using these data, we will employ a Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework, specifically a TOPSIS (Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution) algorithm, to model the 'distance' of each pharmacy from an empirically defined 'non-viable' profile. This will be validated against expert panel assessments of referral necessity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, operationalizable criteria for when pharmacies should refer immunization services, moving beyond identifying barriers to defining actionable thresholds. It will contribute a practical decision-support tool for pharmacy owners and public health planners to allocate resources efficiently and safeguard patient care quality in under-resourced settings.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant research paradigm from 'how to enable' to 'when to refer,' applying a formal multi-criteria decision model (MCDA/TOPSIS) to a pharmacy practice question, which is novel for this field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Development and Pilot Testing of a Low-Cost, Rule-Based Audit Toolkit for Fraud Detection in Independent Rural Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Independent rural pharmacies operate with limited financial and technical resources, making them vulnerable to billing errors and fraud, which can threaten their viability and patient trust. Existing fraud detection systems are often complex, expensive, and designed for large chains, creating a significant implementation gap for small practices. Addressing this gap is critical for ensuring the financial health and regulatory compliance of essential community-based healthcare providers.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified, rule-based audit tool, developed through community-engaged design with independent pharmacists, will be feasible to implement and effective at identifying common, high-risk billing discrepancies in small pharmacy settings.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods implementation science approach. First, we will conduct qualitative interviews and surveys with independent pharmacists to identify key fraud risk areas and resource constraints (Phase 1: Community Assessment). Second, we will co-design a toolkit of simplified audit rules (e.g., for duplicate billing, refill too soon, dose optimization) using logic that can be executed manually or with basic spreadsheet macros (Phase 2: Tool Development). Finally, we will pilot-test the toolkit's feasibility and preliminary efficacy in a cohort of 10-15 independent pharmacies, measuring time-to-audit and potential discrepancy flags (Phase 3: Pilot Implementation).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-access audit toolkit tailored to the resource realities of independent pharmacies, directly addressing a known service gap. It will contribute to the implementation science literature by demonstrating a 'scale-down' approach to fraud prevention, prioritizing accessibility over algorithmic complexity for community pharmacy practice.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior research focused on sophisticated data analytics for large entities, this proposal inverts the paradigm by designing a minimalist, human-centered tool specifically for low-resource settings. It integrates Westrick's expertise in community pharmacy services and implementation barriers with a novel application of basic computational auditing.

proposals.title: Evaluating Inventory Management and Community-Based Dose-Sharing Strategies to Reduce Vaccine Wastage in Independent Rural Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Vaccine wastage due to expiration before use represents a significant financial and public health barrier, particularly for independent community pharmacies in rural areas. These pharmacies face unique challenges, including unpredictable patient demand and logistical constraints, which can lead to discarded doses and reduced willingness to stock vital vaccines. Addressing this wastage is critical for improving pharmacy sustainability and expanding equitable access to immunization services in underserved communities.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a tailored inventory management algorithm combined with a formalized local dose-sharing network among rural pharmacies will significantly reduce vaccine wastage rates compared to standard ordering practices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods, multi-site implementation study. First, we will develop and test a predictive inventory algorithm using historical dispensing data and local population factors (e.g., flu seasonality, school schedules) to generate optimized, pharmacy-specific ordering recommendations. Concurrently, we will design and implement a structured dose-sharing protocol among a network of participating rural pharmacies, facilitated by a secure messaging platform, to reallocate soon-to-expire doses. The primary outcome will be the change in wastage rate (doses expired/doses received) measured over a 12-month intervention period compared to a 12-month baseline.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a practical, scalable toolkit for inventory management tailored to the rural pharmacy context. It will generate novel evidence on the feasibility and impact of formalized local dose-sharing models, contributing directly to the literature on improving operational efficiency and financial viability of essential pharmacy-based public health services.
proposals.novelty: While inventory management studies exist, few integrate predictive analytics specifically for independent rural pharmacies or empirically test structured dose-sharing networks as a coordinated strategy to mitigate expiration, moving beyond anecdotal ad-hoc exchanges.

proposals.title: Co-designing HPV Vaccine Communication Tools for Community Pharmacies: A Human-Centered Design Study with Pharmacists and Parents in Rural Settings
proposals.motivation: HPV vaccine uptake remains suboptimal, particularly in rural areas where pharmacies are critical access points. Pharmacists face communication barriers, including time constraints and parental hesitancy, for which standardized, evidence-based communication aids are lacking. Effective pharmacist-parent communication is essential to improve vaccine confidence and series completion.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a human-centered design (HCD) process with pharmacists and parents will yield communication scripts and materials that are perceived as more usable, acceptable, and effective for discussing the HPV vaccine in community pharmacy settings compared to existing standard materials.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase, qualitative study using established HCD frameworks (e.g., Stanford d.school model). Phase 1: Empathize via interviews/focus groups with rural pharmacists and parents to map communication journeys and barriers. Phase 2: Define key challenges and ideate solutions in co-design workshops. Phase 3: Prototype iterative communication scripts and visual aids. Phase 4: Test prototypes via simulated patient encounters, using thematic analysis of feedback to refine final tools.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, contextually tailored toolkit for HPV vaccine communication in pharmacies, directly addressing implementation gaps. It will advance the field of pharmacy practice by providing a replicable model for applying HCD to clinical service delivery and generate insights into the specific communication needs of rural populations.
proposals.novelty: While HCD is used in healthcare, its application to pharmacist-patient communication, specifically for HPV vaccine in rural pharmacies, is novel. This approach moves beyond surveying barriers to actively co-creating solutions with end-users, integrating Westrick's prior work on linguistic models and rural service implementation.

proposals.title: Assessing the Implementation and Impact of Community Pharmacy-Based HPV Vaccination Programs: A Focus on Series Completion in Rural and Underserved Populations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, pharmacy-based immunization has expanded access, particularly for HPV vaccination. However, public health impact hinges on completing the multi-dose series, a significant challenge in rural areas with documented barriers to care. Current evaluations often prioritize initial uptake, leaving a critical evidence gap on whether pharmacy programs effectively support series completion, which is essential for cancer prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that independent community pharmacies, through tailored patient communication and reminder systems, can achieve HPV vaccine series completion rates comparable to or exceeding traditional medical clinics, especially among rural patients who face greater access barriers.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods implementation study will first survey a national sample of independent community pharmacies to document current practices for tracking and promoting multi-dose series completion. Subsequently, we will conduct a quasi-experimental analysis using de-identified immunization information system (IIS) data, comparing series completion rates for HPV vaccine initiations at participating pharmacies versus local clinics. Statistical analysis will employ multivariate logistic regression, controlling for patient age, gender, and rurality, to isolate the pharmacy effect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first national, practice-based evidence on pharmacy capacity and performance in achieving HPV series completion. It will identify modifiable barriers (e.g., record retrieval, reminder systems) and generate actionable communication strategies, akin to the naloxone linguistic model, to improve follow-up. Findings will inform pharmacy practice standards and public health policy for maximizing the preventive impact of pharmacy-based vaccination.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined pharmacy-based vaccine uptake or IIS use in isolation, this study uniquely shifts the outcome metric to series completion and directly links pharmacy implementation processes to this harder-to-achieve clinical endpoint using robust IIS data.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Feasibility and Impact of an Expanded Pharmacist-Led Annual Wellness Visit Protocol in Rural Community Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Rural populations face significant barriers to accessing primary care, leading to lower rates of preventive screenings and poorer health outcomes. Pharmacists are highly accessible healthcare providers, especially in rural areas, but their role in Annual Wellness Visits (AWVs) is often limited to medication reviews. Expanding their scope to order and interpret a broader range of preventive screenings (e.g., for chronic kidney disease, hepatitis C, or cognitive impairment) could address critical care gaps. This is particularly relevant given pre-2026 trends in provider shortages and value-based care.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, expanded-scope AWV protocol for community pharmacists will be feasible in rural settings and will increase patient-reported completion rates of recommended preventive screenings compared to usual care.
proposals.method: This study will use a mixed-methods, community-based participatory research approach. We will first conduct key informant interviews and focus groups with rural pharmacists, patients, and physicians to co-design an expanded AWV protocol. This will be followed by a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial in 20 independent rural pharmacies (10 intervention, 10 control). The intervention will involve training pharmacists on the new protocol, which includes standardized workflows for ordering specific screenings (e.g., point-of-care tests, lab requisitions) and interpreting results using validated clinical decision support algorithms. Primary outcomes will be feasibility (measured via the Feasibility of Intervention Measure) and patient-reported screening completion at 6 months, analyzed using intention-to-treat multilevel logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a replicable, stakeholder-informed model for expanding pharmacist-provided preventive services. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and potential public health impact of such a model in underserved rural communities, informing policy discussions on pharmacist scope of practice. The findings will contribute directly to implementation science in community pharmacy practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined pharmacist-delivered AWVs, this study uniquely focuses on a protocol that moves beyond medication review to include ordering and interpreting a defined panel of non-traditional preventive screenings, specifically co-designed for and tested in the independent rural pharmacy setting.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Standardized Training Curriculum for Pharmacist-Provided Annual Wellness Visits in Rural Community Settings
proposals.motivation: Annual Wellness Visits (AWVs) are a critical preventive service under Medicare, but access is limited in rural areas where primary care shortages are acute. Pharmacists are well-positioned to fill this gap, yet pre-2026 evidence indicates significant variability in training and confidence, leading to inconsistent service quality and implementation barriers. A standardized, evidence-based training program is needed to ensure rural patients receive high-quality, comprehensive AWVs from community pharmacists.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, multimodal training curriculum for pharmacists, grounded in the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation in Care, will significantly improve pharmacist confidence, knowledge, and the fidelity of AWV delivery compared to existing ad-hoc training methods.
proposals.method: This implementation science study will use a mixed-methods, multi-phase design. First, we will conduct a Delphi panel with experts in pharmacy practice, geriatrics, and health communication to develop the core curriculum modules. The curriculum will then be piloted using a pre-post study with a national sample of independent rural community pharmacists. We will employ validated surveys to measure changes in knowledge and self-efficacy, and conduct structured observations (using a checklist derived from AWV guidelines) of simulated patient encounters to assess fidelity. Qualitative interviews will explore perceived barriers and facilitators to curriculum use.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, publicly available training curriculum tailored for pharmacists, directly addressing a key implementation barrier. It will contribute empirical evidence on effective training strategies for expanding clinical service delivery in community pharmacy, particularly for Medicare beneficiaries in underserved rural areas.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined pharmacist roles in AWVs, no pre-2026 study has developed and rigorously validated a standardized training program specifically designed for the unique communication and clinical documentation needs of this service, integrating linguistic models of patient engagement to improve visit quality.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness and Financial Sustainability of In-Clinic Versus Community Pharmacy-Provided Annual Wellness Visits: A Comparative Analysis for Rural and Underserved Populations
proposals.motivation: The Medicare Annual Wellness Visit (AWV) is a critical preventive service, but access remains a challenge, particularly in rural areas with primary care shortages. Community pharmacies are increasingly proposed as alternative delivery sites, yet the financial viability and comparative cost-effectiveness of in-clinic versus outsourced pharmacy models are poorly understood. Determining the most sustainable model is essential for policymakers and health systems aiming to expand AWV access without compromising fiscal responsibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a community pharmacy-based AWV delivery model will demonstrate superior cost-effectiveness compared to a traditional in-clinic model, particularly in rural settings, when considering total healthcare system costs, patient travel/time burdens, and long-term preventive health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA) from the healthcare system perspective over a 5-year time horizon. Data will be synthesized from: 1) a retrospective cohort analysis of Medicare claims (pre-2026 data) to establish baseline costs and outcomes for in-clinic AWVs; 2) primary data collection on operational costs (staff time, overhead) from a national survey of independent community pharmacies (leveraging Westrick's prior survey methods); and 3) a decision-analytic Markov model to simulate long-term costs and quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) associated with each model, incorporating parameters for screening uptake, travel distance, and rurality. Incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) will be calculated, with sensitivity analyses performed on key parameters.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, system-level economic comparison of AWV delivery models, directly informing payment policy for pharmacist-provided services. It will offer actionable data for community pharmacy owners on the financial sustainability of offering AWVs and for health systems considering partnerships. The findings will advance implementation science by quantifying economic barriers and facilitators to integrating pharmacies into preventive care pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined patient satisfaction or plan selection in pharmacy counseling, this study uniquely integrates health services survey methods with advanced decision-analytic modeling to perform a full economic evaluation, explicitly modeling the impact of rurality and pharmacy operational costs—a gap in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Implementation of Pharmacist-Provided Annual Wellness Visits in Rural Independent Pharmacies: A Mixed-Methods Study Using the RE-AIM Framework
proposals.motivation: The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Annual Wellness Visit (AWV) is a critical preventive service, yet access remains limited in rural areas. Pharmacists are uniquely positioned to deliver AWVs, but the implementation of such services in independent community pharmacies, particularly in rural settings, is poorly understood. Systematic evaluation is needed to identify effective strategies and persistent barriers to sustainable service integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the RE-AIM (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance) framework will reveal that successful implementation of pharmacist AWV programs in rural independent pharmacies is primarily limited by practice-level adoption barriers (e.g., workflow, reimbursement) and patient-level reach barriers (e.g., awareness, transportation), rather than clinical effectiveness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a sequential explanatory mixed-methods study. First, a national survey of independent pharmacy owners/managers will quantitatively assess RE-AIM dimensions. Second, qualitative interviews with a purposive sample of survey respondents (pharmacists) and patients from rural pharmacies will explore implementation barriers and facilitators. Analysis will involve descriptive and inferential statistics for survey data and thematic analysis using the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR) to code interview transcripts, integrating findings within the RE-AIM structure.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a structured, framework-based evaluation of a real-world clinical service expansion, providing actionable data for pharmacy owners, payers, and policymakers. It will contribute a validated assessment tool and a detailed playbook for implementing AWVs in rural community pharmacy practice, directly addressing known gaps in preventive care access.
proposals.novelty: While implementation science frameworks are used in health services research, their application to the specific, emerging practice of pharmacist-conducted AWVs, with a dedicated focus on the rural independent pharmacy setting, is novel. This work integrates Westrick's expertise in pharmacy services, rural health, and implementation barriers with a formal implementation science methodology.

proposals.title: Applying Marketing Analytics for Targeted Outreach: A Community Pharmacy-Based Intervention to Increase Annual Wellness Visit Uptake in Rural Medicare Beneficiaries
proposals.motivation: Annual Wellness Visits (AWVs) are a critical preventive service for Medicare beneficiaries, yet uptake remains suboptimal, particularly in rural areas with primary care access barriers. Community pharmacies are trusted, accessible healthcare nodes but are underutilized for proactive patient outreach. Pre-2026, marketing analytics have successfully driven consumer behavior in retail but are rarely applied systematically by pharmacies to improve specific health service utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: A community pharmacy-led outreach intervention, using customer segmentation and targeted messaging informed by marketing analytics, will significantly increase scheduled AWV appointments among eligible rural Medicare patients compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in 40 independent rural pharmacies. Using de-identified pharmacy claims data (e.g., medication fills, demographics), we will apply a two-stage analytical approach: 1) Segmentation via k-means clustering and CHAID decision trees to identify distinct patient subgroups (e.g., 'high-risk chronic', 'preventive-engaged'). 2) A/B testing of tailored outreach messages (mail, phone, SMS) designed for each segment, with message framing informed by the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation. The primary outcome is the proportion of eligible patients scheduling an AWV within 3 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a replicable, data-driven framework for pharmacies to conduct targeted health outreach, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches. It will generate evidence on the effectiveness and cost-efficiency of applying commercial marketing techniques to a clinical preventive service in a rural, pharmacy-based setting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined pharmacy-based immunization or naloxone outreach, the novel integration of formal marketing analytics (segmentation, A/B testing) specifically to drive AWV uptake represents a significant methodological advance for pharmacy services research.

proposals.title: Leveraging the Annual Wellness Visit in Community Pharmacies to Identify and Recruit Rural Older Adults for Preventive Clinical Trials: A Feasibility and Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: Preventive clinical trials for older adults, especially in rural areas, face significant recruitment challenges, leading to delays and underrepresentation. The Medicare Annual Wellness Visit (AWV) is a structured, reimbursable encounter that systematically assesses health status, risk factors, and preventive service needs, creating a potential, untapped recruitment pipeline. Community pharmacists are increasingly providing AWVs and are trusted, accessible healthcare providers in rural communities, positioning them uniquely to bridge this recruitment gap.
proposals.hypothesis: A standardized protocol embedded within the community pharmacy-based AWV will be a feasible and effective method for identifying, pre-screening, and recruiting eligible rural older adults into preventive clinical trials (e.g., for cognitive decline, osteoporosis, cardiovascular health).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, mixed-methods implementation study in 10-15 independent rural community pharmacies. Pharmacists will be trained to integrate a brief clinical trial screening module (using validated eligibility checklists) into their existing AWV workflow. Quantitative data (recruitment yield, patient demographics, screening time) will be tracked via a secure REDCap database. Qualitative data from pharmacist interviews and patient surveys will assess barriers (e.g., time, workflow, communication) and facilitators using the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR).
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a novel, practical recruitment model that leverages an existing preventive service (AWV) and an underutilized provider (pharmacist) to enhance clinical trial access for rural populations. It will produce an implementation toolkit, including workflow maps and communication scripts, to guide future trial recruitment in community pharmacy settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined AWV delivery and pharmacy-based clinical services separately, no pre-2026 research has explicitly designed and tested the AWV as a systematic recruitment tool for external clinical trials, nor has it focused on the unique role of rural community pharmacists in this recruitment paradigm.

proposals.title: Mapping the Black Box of MTM: A Mixed-Methods Study of Encounter Heterogeneity and Patient Outcomes in Rural Community Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Medication Therapy Management (MTM) is a cornerstone service for improving medication use, yet its real-world delivery is poorly characterized, especially in rural settings with unique access challenges. Prior research often treats MTM as a uniform intervention, masking critical variation in how pharmacists implement it, which may explain inconsistent findings on its effectiveness. Understanding this heterogeneity is essential to optimize service delivery and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, measurable dimensions of MTM encounter structure (e.g., duration, communication style) and content (e.g., topic coverage) will be significantly associated with differential patient outcomes, including medication adherence and healthcare utilization.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design in a network of rural community pharmacies. Objective encounter data will be captured via audio recording and coded using the Roter Interaction Analysis System (RIAS) and a structured topic checklist. Subjective data will come from post-encounter surveys of patients and pharmacists. These multidimensional features will be analyzed against outcomes (e.g., PDC for adherence, claims data for hospitalizations) using multilevel regression and latent class analysis to identify clusters of effective encounter profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed taxonomy of real-world MTM encounter types, moving the field beyond a binary 'MTM vs. no MTM' paradigm. It will identify which specific, modifiable components of pharmacist-patient interaction are most strongly linked to success, offering actionable targets for training and reimbursement policy.
proposals.novelty: This work directly challenges the false assumption of MTM uniformity by applying a linguistic and behavioral framework to deconstruct the service, integrating objective communication analysis with patient-reported experience and hard outcomes—an approach not yet applied in rural pharmacy practice research.

proposals.title: Testing the Motivation-Confounding Hypothesis in Medication Therapy Management: A Multi-Country Comparative Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Evaluations of Medication Therapy Management (MTM) programs in the United States are persistently challenged by potential confounding by unobserved patient motivation, as more motivated patients may self-select into MTM and also have better outcomes. This assumption, if universally true, undermines causal claims about MTM's effectiveness and stifles program innovation. A direct test of this foundational assumption is needed to advance the methodological rigor of pharmacy services research.
proposals.hypothesis: If unobserved patient motivation is the primary confounder in MTM evaluations, then matched cohorts of non-MTM patients across different healthcare systems should demonstrate similar clinical outcomes. Significant international variation in outcomes for comparable non-MTM patients would challenge the universality of the motivation-confounding hypothesis.
proposals.method: We will employ a retrospective, comparative cohort design using linked administrative claims and pharmacy data from 2019-2024 for patients with polypharmacy in the US, Canada (Ontario), and the UK. Cohorts will be matched using high-dimensional propensity scores on demographics, clinical complexity, and medication regimens. The primary analysis will compare the incidence of potentially inappropriate prescribing (using explicit criteria like Beers or STOPP) and medication-related hospitalizations between 1) MTM-eligible patients in the US and matched patients in other systems, and 2) non-MTM patients across all three systems. Difference-in-differences and instrumental variable analyses will test for effect modification by system-level factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a key causal inference assumption in MTM research, moving beyond descriptive international comparison. It will generate evidence on how system-level design (eligibility rules, pharmacist integration, prescribing culture) independently influences medication safety, offering actionable insights for optimizing MTM implementation, particularly in rural and community pharmacy settings.
proposals.novelty: This approach innovatively leverages natural international variation as a methodological tool to isolate and test a specific confounding hypothesis, a novel application in pharmacy services research. It shifts the focus from simply comparing MTM outcomes to deconstructing the assumptions that hinder their evaluation.

proposals.title: Long-Term Causal Effects of Medication Therapy Management on Rural Patient Outcomes: A Staggered Difference-in-Differences Analysis
proposals.motivation: Medication Therapy Management (MTM) services are a cornerstone of pharmacist-led care, yet evidence for their long-term, causal impact on patient outcomes remains limited, especially in rural settings with distinct access barriers. Pre-2026 studies often rely on pre-post designs vulnerable to confounding by secular trends in healthcare delivery and policy. Robustly quantifying MTM's sustained value is critical for justifying pharmacist reimbursement and scaling effective services to underserved populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained enrollment in comprehensive MTM services causes a significant reduction in total cost of care and hospitalizations over a five-year period for rural Medicare beneficiaries with multiple chronic conditions, relative to a matched control cohort not receiving MTM.
proposals.method: We will implement a staggered, longitudinal difference-in-differences (DiD) design using Medicare claims data (2015-2024). Patients newly enrolling in MTM programs at independent community pharmacies form treated cohorts across staggered years. A control cohort will be constructed via propensity score matching on demographics, clinical risk scores, and baseline healthcare utilization from similar rural regions without MTM access. The primary analysis will use the two-way fixed effects estimator with robust standard errors, clustering at the patient level, to compare outcome trajectories. Sensitivity analyses will include the Callaway and Sant'Anna estimator to address potential biases from heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first quasi-experimental, causal estimates of MTM's long-term economic and clinical value, specifically for rural patients. It will advance methods for evaluating pharmacy-based service implementation by demonstrating a robust design to isolate intervention effects from background trends. Findings will inform policy on sustainable MTM reimbursement models.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond cross-sectional surveys and simple pre-post comparisons by applying a staggered DiD design—a cutting-edge causal inference method in health services research pre-2026—to the specific context of pharmacist-delivered MTM in rural communities, directly addressing temporal confounding.

proposals.title: Estimating the Causal Effect of Medication Therapy Management on Rural Patient Outcomes: An Instrumental Variable Analysis Using Policy Discontinuities
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the true impact of Medication Therapy Management (MTM) is critical for optimizing pharmacist-led services, especially in rural areas with high healthcare needs. However, standard observational studies are severely confounded because patients who enroll in MTM differ systematically from those who do not, biasing estimates of program effectiveness. A more rigorous causal estimate is needed to inform policy and resource allocation for community pharmacy services.
proposals.hypothesis: Using a plausibly exogenous instrumental variable for MTM enrollment, such as a sharp discontinuity in Medicare Part D MTM eligibility thresholds, will reveal a significant causal reduction in hospitalizations and emergency department visits among rural patients, an effect underestimated by conventional regression methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) instrumental variable framework, analogous to Mendelian randomization in epidemiology. The instrument will be an indicator for patients just above versus just below the annual drug cost and medication count thresholds for mandatory Medicare Part D MTM enrollment. Using a national pharmacy claims dataset (e.g., Medicare Part A/B/D claims linked to MTM records from 2019-2024), we will first model the probability of MTM receipt based on the instrument, controlling for demographic covariates. In the second stage, we will regress healthcare utilization outcomes (e.g., hospital admissions) on the predicted MTM participation from the first stage.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first quasi-experimental, causal estimates of MTM's impact on hard clinical endpoints, directly addressing a major methodological limitation in pharmacy services research. The findings will offer robust evidence to guide the design and targeting of MTM programs, particularly for independent community pharmacies serving rural populations.
proposals.novelty: While instrumental variables are established in economics and epidemiology, their application to evaluate pharmacist-provided MTM services, using policy rules as instruments, is novel. This approach moves beyond describing associations or barriers to rigorously isolating causality from observational pharmacy data.

proposals.title: Causal Drivers and Community Pharmacist Perspectives on Proton Pump Inhibitor Utilization in Rural Older Adults: A Mixed-Methods Study
proposals.motivation: Proton pump inhibitor (PPI) overuse, particularly among older adults, is a persistent clinical and policy concern linked to adverse outcomes like fractures and Clostridioides difficile infection. Rural patients may face distinct drivers of utilization, including limited access to specialist care and diagnostic services, but causal evidence is lacking. Understanding the specific factors and mechanisms driving PPI use in this subgroup is critical for designing effective, targeted deprescribing interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific policy changes (e.g., Medicare Part D formulary shifts) and increased direct-to-consumer advertising are causal drivers of rising long-term PPI use in rural older adults, and that these drivers operate through identifiable clinical and behavioral pathways involving both prescribers and patients.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, using national pharmacy claims data (2015-2025), we will apply a difference-in-differences quasi-experimental approach to quantify the causal impact of policy and marketing events on PPI initiation and continuation rates in rural versus urban older adult subgroups. Second, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with community pharmacists, primary care providers, and patients in rural areas to explore the decision-making mechanisms (e.g., diagnostic uncertainty, patient demand, refill protocols) underlying the quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal, rather than associative, evidence linking specific external factors to PPI utilization in a high-risk, underserved population. It will generate a mechanistic explanation of how these factors influence real-world prescribing and medication use behaviors, directly informing the design of pharmacist-led stewardship programs tailored to rural community settings.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond descriptive epidemiology by integrating robust quasi-experimental causal inference with qualitative exploration of mechanisms, a novel approach in pharmacy practice research. It uniquely centers the community pharmacist's perspective as a key informant on the pathways from policy and marketing to patient-level medication use.

proposals.title: Predictors of Guideline-Concordant Non-Use of Pharmacist-Provided Immunizations in Rural Community Pharmacies: A Multi-Method Analysis
proposals.motivation: Despite clear guidelines and expanded authority for pharmacists to administer vaccines, significant gaps in immunization coverage persist, particularly in rural areas. Understanding why patients with clear indications do not receive pharmacist-provided immunizations (PPIs) is critical for improving public health outcomes and addressing rural health disparities. This 'inverse problem' focuses on non-use, a key barrier to achieving population-level immunity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-use of PPIs among guideline-eligible patients is associated with a multi-level confluence of factors, including patient-level (e.g., cost concerns, vaccine hesitancy), pharmacy-level (e.g., service promotion, workflow), and community-level (e.g., rurality, access to alternatives) barriers.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent parallel mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will analyze de-identified patient and pharmacy data from a national pharmacy chain (2019-2024) using logistic regression and machine learning (e.g., random forest) to identify predictors of non-use among eligible patients. Qualitatively, we will conduct semi-structured interviews and focus groups with patients and pharmacists in selected rural pharmacies, using thematic analysis to contextualize quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, multi-level model of PPI non-use, moving beyond simple descriptions of service availability. It will generate actionable evidence for designing targeted interventions to improve immunization uptake in rural community pharmacies and inform policy to reduce implementation barriers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines facilitators of service implementation, this study uniquely applies an 'inverse problem' lens to patient-level non-use within a rural pharmacy context, integrating robust pharmacy data with community-engaged qualitative methods to uncover root causes.

proposals.title: Beyond Policy Enactment: A Mixed-Methods Study of Individual-Level Barriers to Pharmacist Engagement with Naloxone Standing Orders in Rural Communities
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread adoption of naloxone standing orders to expand access, persistent gaps in rural pharmacy-based naloxone dispensing exist. Prior research has focused on policy and systems-level factors, but the critical role of individual pharmacist attitudes and self-efficacy remains underexplored, particularly in rural settings where pharmacists are frontline public health providers. Understanding these individual-level barriers is essential to translate policy into effective practice and address rural health disparities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among rural community pharmacists, individual-level factors (e.g., stigma, confidence in patient communication, perceived role legitimacy) will be stronger predictors of naloxone dispensing under standing orders than pharmacy-level structural characteristics.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a national cross-sectional survey of independent rural community pharmacists will measure dispensing frequency, stigma (using adapted scales), communication confidence, and practice environment factors. Survey data will be analyzed using hierarchical multiple regression. Second, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with a purposive sample of 20-30 pharmacists stratified by survey-reported engagement levels. Interviews will be analyzed using thematic analysis guided by the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation in Care to understand communication barriers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, granular understanding of the human factors limiting naloxone access in rural America. It will generate actionable evidence for designing targeted interventions (e.g., communication skills training, stigma reduction modules) that complement policy efforts. Findings will directly inform the implementation science of pharmacy-based public health services.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the dominant policy-focused research paradigm by centering the individual pharmacist's experience. It uniquely integrates quantitative measurement of stigma/confidence with a qualitative, communication-theory-driven exploration of barriers, specifically within the understudied rural independent pharmacy context.

proposals.title: Expanding the Scope of Pharmacy-Based Public Health: A Mixed-Methods Assessment of Training Needs for Technicians and Students in Rural Community Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: Community pharmacies, especially in rural areas, are critical access points for public health services like immunizations, naloxone distribution, and point-of-care testing. Pre-2026 research, including Westrick's work, often focuses training and implementation efforts on pharmacists, creating a potential bottleneck. This overlooks the integral role of pharmacy technicians and students in service delivery and patient communication, which may limit the efficiency, reach, and sustainability of these expanded services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacy technicians and students in rural community pharmacies have distinct, unmet training needs related to public health service implementation, and that addressing these needs through tailored training will improve reported team efficacy and reduce perceived barriers to service delivery.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a national survey of independent community pharmacy staff (pharmacists, technicians, students) will quantify perceived barriers, training experiences, and self-efficacy for specific services (e.g., IIS retrieval, point-of-care testing). Survey data will be analyzed using multivariate regression to identify role-specific predictors. Second, we will conduct focus groups with rural pharmacy teams, informed by the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation in Care, to explore qualitative themes around task delegation, communication, and training gaps. Thematic analysis will integrate findings to develop a role-tailored training framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first national, role-specific analysis of training needs within pharmacy teams for public health services. It will contribute a practical framework for designing team-based training programs that leverage all personnel, potentially increasing service capacity and addressing rural health disparities. Findings will inform pharmacy education, accreditation standards, and health system implementation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies pharmacists' needs, this study explicitly challenges the assumption that the pharmacist is the sole target for advanced service training. It novelly applies a team-based lens and communication models to the implementation science of pharmacy-based public health, focusing on the understudied roles of technicians and students in rural settings.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Impact of Enhanced Communication Training on Pharmacist Comfort in Patient Counseling: A Mixed-Methods Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: Pharmacists are increasingly responsible for direct patient care services, such as naloxone dispensing, immunization, and medication therapy management, particularly in underserved rural areas. Pre-2026 research by Westrick and others identifies communication as a key barrier to effective service implementation. While continuing education (CE) often includes communication content, its direct impact on pharmacist self-efficacy and comfort is not rigorously measured, creating a gap between training and practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Pharmacists who complete a CE course with enhanced, structured communication training based on models like the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation in Care will report a statistically significant increase in self-reported comfort levels when counseling patients on sensitive or complex topics, compared to a control group receiving standard CE content.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, pragmatic controlled trial with independent community pharmacists. The intervention is a novel CE module integrating role-play simulations and Westrick's previously developed 'practical communication tips' for sensitive topics. Pharmacists will be randomized to receive this enhanced training or a standard knowledge-based CE control. The primary outcome is the change in a validated Pharmacist Counseling Comfort Scale (PCCS) score from pre-test to 4-week post-test. Secondary outcomes include qualitative analysis of post-intervention focus groups to explore perceived barriers and facilitators to applying the training in practice, particularly in rural settings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of communication-focused training for pharmacists, moving beyond knowledge assessment to measure a key psychological precursor to behavior change. It will yield a tested training module and implementation strategy that pharmacy organizations can adopt to improve service delivery, especially for critical public health roles like opioid overdose prevention and immunization counseling.
proposals.novelty: While communication is acknowledged as important, this study uniquely applies a specific linguistic framework to CE design and quantitatively tests its impact on pharmacist comfort—a proximal outcome to actual counseling behavior—rather than just knowledge or satisfaction, which is a novel approach in pharmacy practice research.

proposals.title: A Dual-Perspective Analysis of Communication Barriers in Rural Community Pharmacies: Pharmacist Discomfort and Patient/Caregiver Perceptions
proposals.motivation: In rural settings, community pharmacists are critical access points for health services, yet communication barriers can impede effective care delivery, such as naloxone counseling or immunization coordination. Prior research often examines barriers from either the provider or patient perspective in isolation, creating an incomplete picture. A simultaneous, dyadic analysis is needed to identify actionable gaps and improve service implementation in these under-resourced areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant communication gap exists where pharmacist-reported discomfort in initiating sensitive health conversations (e.g., substance use, mental health) is misaligned with patient/caregiver expectations and perceptions of pharmacist roles, and that this misalignment is more pronounced in rural independent pharmacies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a convergent parallel mixed-methods study in a stratified sample of 20 rural independent pharmacies. First, we will administer validated surveys to pharmacists (assessing communication discomfort, self-efficacy) and a matched sample of their patients/caregivers (assessing perceptions, expectations). Second, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with pharmacist-patient dyads. Quantitative data will be analyzed using descriptive statistics and paired t-tests to compare perspectives, while qualitative data will undergo thematic analysis using the Linguistic Model of Patient Participation in Care as a framework. The two strands will be integrated during interpretation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, dyadic model of pharmacy communication barriers, identifying specific, misaligned domains that hinder service delivery. The findings will directly inform the development of a dual-perspective communication training toolkit for pharmacists and patient-facing materials to align expectations, thereby enhancing the implementation of clinical services in rural pharmacies.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond single-perspective studies by concurrently capturing and comparing the views of both members of the pharmacist-patient dyad within the same clinical encounter context, a method not commonly applied in pharmacy practice research. It uniquely applies a linguistic communication model to analyze the structural and perceptual roots of barriers in rural independent settings.

proposals.title: Mapping and Strengthening the Pharmacy-Public Health Network for Rural Emergency Preparedness: A Social Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Rural communities face significant health disparities and rely heavily on community pharmacies for essential services, especially during public health emergencies. Pre-2026 research highlights communication gaps between pharmacies and public health agencies as a critical barrier to coordinated response for events like opioid overdoses, infectious disease outbreaks, and natural disasters. Understanding the structure and quality of these communication ties is a necessary first step to building more resilient rural health systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rural pharmacies have weaker and less formalized communication networks with local and state public health agencies compared to their urban counterparts, and that targeted interventions based on network analysis can identify and strengthen these critical weak ties.
proposals.method: We will conduct a national survey of community pharmacy managers (independent and chain) to collect egocentric network data on their communication frequency, mode, and perceived quality with local health departments, state immunization information systems, and emergency management. Using UCINET or Gephi software, we will compute network-level metrics (density, centrality, brokerage) and node-level metrics (degree, betweenness) to map the structure. Exponential random graph models (ERGMs) will be used to test which pharmacy characteristics (e.g., rurality, type) predict network position.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of pharmacy-public health communication networks for emergency response, identifying specific structural vulnerabilities in rural areas. It will contribute a replicable methodology for assessing coordination infrastructure and generate evidence-based recommendations for targeted interventions to strengthen these networks, directly informing public health policy and pharmacy practice.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been used in healthcare, its application to the specific, operational interface between community pharmacies and public health agencies for emergency preparedness is novel. This work moves beyond identifying individual-level barriers to analyzing the systemic, relational architecture that enables or hinders coordinated action.

proposals.title: Co-designing Adolescent Vaccination Services: A Human-Centered Design Study with Rural Pharmacists and Parents
proposals.motivation: Adolescent vaccination rates for HPV, meningococcal, and Tdap remain suboptimal, with disparities exacerbated in rural areas where pharmacies are critical access points. Pre-2026 research identifies barriers including adolescent discomfort, privacy concerns, and logistical challenges, but service models are rarely designed with end-user input. Applying human-centered design (HCD) directly within the pharmacy setting offers a novel pathway to create more accessible and acceptable services.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured co-design process involving rural community pharmacists and parents of adolescents will generate feasible, context-specific service prototypes that address key psychosocial and logistical barriers to vaccination, leading to higher perceived acceptability and usability compared to standard pharmacy models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase HCD study in three rural regions. Phase 1: Empathize via contextual inquiries (pharmacy observations) and semi-structured interviews with 10-15 pharmacists and 15-20 parents. Phase 2: Define through affinity diagramming and journey mapping to synthesize barriers and opportunities. Phase 3: Ideate and Prototype in a series of three facilitated co-design workshops with separate then combined parent and pharmacist groups, using brainstorming techniques and low-fidelity prototyping tools. Phase 4: Test refined service prototypes via simulated scenarios and structured feedback surveys.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, replicable HCD toolkit for pharmacy service redesign and a set of actionable, adolescent-friendly vaccination service blueprints tailored for the rural community pharmacy context. It will advance implementation science by demonstrating how participatory design can bridge the gap between identified barriers and practical, locally-owned solutions in pharmacy practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has surveyed barriers or tested specific interventions, applying a formal co-design methodology from human-computer interaction to pharmacy service creation is novel. It moves beyond identifying problems to collaboratively generating solutions, directly engaging pharmacists and parents as design partners rather than just study subjects.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Coordinated Physician-Pharmacist HPV Vaccination Model: A Hybrid Implementation-Effectiveness Trial in Rural Communities
proposals.motivation: Despite the proven efficacy of the HPV vaccine, completion rates, particularly in rural areas, remain suboptimal due to fragmented care, access barriers, and missed clinical opportunities. Pharmacists are increasingly authorized to administer vaccines, but their integration into a systematic, physician-recommended care pathway is underdeveloped. This study addresses a critical gap in scaling preventive health interventions by leveraging the entire healthcare ecosystem to improve series completion.
proposals.hypothesis: A coordinated system where primary care physicians systematically recommend and refer patients to community pharmacies for HPV vaccine administration will significantly increase series initiation and completion rates among eligible rural patients compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a hybrid type 2 implementation-effectiveness cluster-randomized trial in rural regions. Participating primary care clinics will be randomized to intervention (integrated referral protocol) or control (standard care). The intervention uses a standardized, EHR-integrated referral algorithm to generate patient-specific recommendations and direct electronic notifications to partnered community pharmacies. Pharmacies will implement a structured protocol for scheduling, administration, and documentation feedback to the physician's office. Primary outcomes are HPV vaccine series initiation/completion (effectiveness) and measures of feasibility, fidelity, and pharmacist/physician satisfaction (implementation).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a replicable model for scaling vaccination services through cross-setting clinical integration, directly addressing rural health disparities. It will generate evidence on the operational workflows, communication barriers, and economic costs of implementing a coordinated pharmacist-physician intervention, informing policy and payment models for team-based care.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined pharmacist-administered vaccines or physician recommendations in isolation, this study tests a *systematically coordinated* bidirectional workflow, moving beyond siloed interventions to a true ecosystem approach. It uniquely applies implementation science frameworks to the specific challenge of inter-provider coordination for preventive service scale-up.

proposals.title: Beyond the Prescription Counter: A Comparative Analysis of Trust Brokers for Rural Health Interventions
proposals.motivation: Rural health initiatives increasingly rely on pharmacists as local trust brokers for services like naloxone distribution, immunization, and Medicare counseling. This reliance is based on their accessibility and clinical expertise, but it risks overlooking other community-based actors who may be more effective or appropriate for specific health topics. A systematic comparison is needed to ensure resource allocation and program design are optimized for diverse rural health challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: The effectiveness of a local trust broker for a rural health intervention is contingent on the specific health topic and community context; therefore, pharmacists will not be the optimal broker across all topics when compared to other candidates like community health workers, librarians, or faith leaders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, mixed-methods study in 8-12 diverse rural U.S. counties. First, a discrete choice experiment (DCE) survey will quantify patient preferences for different broker types (pharmacist, community health worker, etc.) across health topics (substance use, preventive care, chronic disease). Second, semi-structured interviews and focus groups with community members and brokers will explore contextual factors influencing trust. Analysis will employ hierarchical Bayesian modeling for the DCE data and thematic analysis for qualitative data, integrating findings to map topic-specific broker effectiveness.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical framework for matching health topics to optimal local trust brokers in rural settings, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach. It will offer actionable guidance for public health agencies and policymakers designing community-embedded interventions to maximize reach and impact.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines pharmacists' role in specific services, this study directly challenges the assumed primacy of the pharmacist by conducting a head-to-head, topic-specific comparison with other community-based actors, using a robust preference-elicitation methodology.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Remote and In-Person Recruitment Strategies for Rural Community Pharmacy-Based Patient Engagement
proposals.motivation: Recruiting rural patients for pharmacy-based services and research is challenging due to geographic dispersion, travel burden, and limited digital access. Pre-2026 literature highlights these barriers but lacks direct, systematic comparisons of recruitment modalities. Determining the most effective and cost-efficient strategy is critical for expanding the reach of essential pharmacy services like immunization, naloxone distribution, and chronic disease management in underserved areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-modal remote recruitment strategy (combining telephone, text, and mailed invitations) will yield comparable participant enrollment rates to traditional in-person recruitment at the pharmacy counter, but at a lower cost per enrolled participant, for a rural health service intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial across 40 independent rural community pharmacies. Pharmacies will be randomized to use either a remote or in-person protocol to recruit patients for a Medicare Part D counseling service. The remote arm will use an algorithm to identify eligible patients from pharmacy records, followed by a sequenced protocol of automated text messages, scripted phone calls, and mailed letters. The in-person arm will use a standardized script for pharmacists to recruit patients during visits. Primary outcomes are recruitment yield (proportion enrolled) and cost-per-enrollment, analyzed using mixed-effects logistic and linear regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, head-to-head evidence on the efficacy and cost of recruitment strategies specifically for rural pharmacy-patient engagement. It will generate a practical, tested protocol for rural recruitment that can be adapted for other pharmacy-based public health services, directly informing implementation science in community pharmacy practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined rural barriers and compared counseling modes, no study has experimentally tested recruitment strategies in this setting. This directly bridges Westrick's expertise in rural pharmacy services, patient communication, and comparative effectiveness methods into a novel experimental design.

proposals.title: Measuring the Pharmacist-Research Trust Transfer: A Mixed-Methods Study of Rural Patient Willingness to Engage in Pharmacy-Based Public Health Research
proposals.motivation: Rural communities face significant health disparities and are often underrepresented in clinical and public health research. Community pharmacists are among the most accessible and trusted healthcare professionals in these settings, as evidenced by their central role in services like naloxone distribution and immunization. Leveraging this established trust is a promising but under-quantified strategy to improve rural research participation and address health equity gaps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a patient's reported trust in their local community pharmacist will be a significant, positive predictor of their willingness to participate in a pharmacist-facilitated research study, and that this trust transfer effect will be moderated by the perceived alignment of the research with the pharmacy's existing care services.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a national survey of patients from independent community pharmacies will quantify trust (using a validated Pharmacist Trust Scale) and research willingness (using discrete choice experiments). Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) will test the trust-transfer pathway and moderating variables.
