ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Community Decomposition to Analyze PPI Agent Expenditure
ideas.description: This idea adapts the additive partitioning method from community ecology to the economic analysis of pharmaceutical expenditure. In ecology, total biodiversity change is decomposed into within-habitat (alpha) and between-habitat (beta) components. Analogously, we decompose the total change in expenditure on Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs) into a 'within-class effect' (capturing price and volume changes for existing agents within a therapeutic class or formulary tier) and a 'mix or between-class effect' (capturing the expenditure impact of shifting patient or prescription volume toward different classes, brands, or generic statuses). This provides a clearer attribution of cost drivers, separating pure price/volume dynamics from structural changes in the agent mix.

ideas.title: Dynamic Agent-Based Decomposition: Integrating Strategic Simulation with Structural Index Analysis for PPI
ideas.description: This research integrates agent-based modeling (ABM) with structural index decomposition analysis to overcome the static categorization flaw. An ABM simulates the healthcare market, where PPI agents (e.g., hospitals, insurers) interact strategically based on behavioral rules, generating dynamic pathways for expenditure changes. The simulated outcomes are then fed into a decomposition framework to rigorously quantify the contributions of price, volume, and mix effects from these emergent behaviors. This hybrid approach allows for causal testing of policy interventions by observing simulated strategic responses before applying the decomposition to real or simulated outcome data.

ideas.title: Dynamic Horizon Analysis for MTM Return: Data-Driven Identification of Effect Maturation and Decay
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a fixed study period to dynamically determine the relevant effect window for MTM interventions. It uses statistical breakpoint analysis and impulse response functions to empirically identify the onset, peak, and decay of economic returns within a longer panel dataset. The system categorizes interventions by type and context, modeling heterogeneous effect durations and testing for long-term or cyclical re-emergence of impacts. This creates a robust, evidence-based understanding of the true economic return timeline, overcoming the arbitrariness of any pre-specified window.

ideas.title: A Federated Transportability Framework for Scaling MTM Analysis Across Heterogeneous National Payer Data
ideas.description: This study scales the MTM analysis to a large national claims database by directly addressing the critical flaw of non-transportability across heterogeneous employer groups. It employs a federated analytic approach that first assesses between-employer heterogeneity in key study parameters before synthesis. Using transportability and meta-analytic techniques, it formally models this heterogeneity to produce generalizable estimates for a defined target population or clearly stratified results. This redesigned system ensures robust, interpretable findings that acknowledge and account for the structural variability inherent in multi-employer data.

ideas.title: Applying Ecosystem Services Valuation to Employer-Centric MTM
ideas.description: This idea inverts the perspective on MTM value to be fully employer-centric by adapting a solution from environmental economics. The analogous problem—valuing ecosystems beyond direct market outputs—was solved through ecosystem services valuation, which monetizes indirect benefits like water purification and climate regulation. We adapt this by treating the employer's workforce and operational environment as an 'ecosystem.' The research will develop a valuation framework that quantifies and monetizes the full spectrum of MTM benefits to the employer: direct medical cost savings (akin to provisioning services), the reduction in informal caregiver burden which stabilizes the workforce (supporting services), and the avoidance of productivity loss due to absenteeism/presenteeism (regulating services). The output is a 'Total Employer Value' metric that fully captures societal costs currently externalized, providing a more compelling business case for employer investment in MTM programs.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to Capture Long-Term MTM Returns
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that MTM's full economic return materializes within a short-term window by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, studying ecosystem succession requires long-term plots and models for delayed, non-linear effects. The analogous solution involves longitudinal tracking and stage-based models. Adapted to MTM research, this means designing a longitudinal, stage-based framework that tracks leading indicators and models delayed economic returns as cascading effects through the organizational ecosystem, conceptualizing the post-training period as a succession of capability development and value realization stages, rather than assuming a fixed observation period captures all relevant outcomes.
